topic: rant about DJs who use pre-recorded sets

turns.content: I need to VENT rn because I just watched a DJ perform at a club last night and fam that set was 100% PRE-RECORDED. not a single live transition. the whole ting was a bounce file. I'm HEATED!!
Ha, that's a hot topic in the DJ world. What tipped you off that it was pre-recorded? Was it obvious to the crowd or just to you as someone who knows what to look for?

okay so FIRST OF ALL the transitions were TOO perfect. like inhuman timing, no slight adjustments, nothing. second the EQ knobs weren't doing ANYTHING — I watched closely and the levels on the mixer weren't moving when the person was turning knobs. and third!! they stepped away from the booth for like 2 minutes and NOTHING changed. the mix kept going perfectly!!

Ha, the walking-away-from-the-booth test is the ultimate giveaway. If the DJ leaves and the music doesn't skip a beat, that's a pre-recorded set playing off a USB or laptop.

The fake knob-turning is almost worse though — at least own it if you're going to do it. Pretending to mix while a file plays is basically a performance of deception.

Is this a well-known DJ or someone local?

I'm not gonna name names but they have a DECENT following and they're getting booked at good venues. and that's what makes me MAD. because actual DJs who spend hours practicing beatmatching and reading crowds and building sets in real time are getting passed over for people who just press PLAY and look good for the gram

That frustration is completely valid. There's a real tension in the industry right now between DJing as a craft and DJing as an image/brand. Some promoters honestly don't care if the set is live — they care about the name, the following, the Instagram aesthetic, the ability to sell tickets.

And that sucks for technically skilled DJs who are actually doing the work. It devalues the craft.

But here's the thing — the audience that matters CAN tell the difference. The heads in the crowd, other DJs, the people who go out specifically for good music? They know. And word gets around.

but thats the PROBLEM!! most of the crowd CANT tell. they just hear good music and see someone standing behind a deck looking cool. they don't know the difference between a live mix and a wav file!! so the fake DJs keep getting booked because the crowd doesnt care

You're right — most casual clubgoers genuinely cannot tell the difference and don't care. They're there for a good time, not to evaluate technical skill. That's just the reality.

But I'd push back a little bit: the reason to mix live isn't just for the audience's benefit. It's because:

1. **You can read the room** — adjust energy in real time, drop a track when you see the crowd responding to a vibe
2. **Happy accidents** — some of the best moments in a set come from spontaneous decisions, unexpected blends, taking risks
3. **It keeps YOU engaged** — performing is more fulfilling when you're actually performing
4. **Longevity** — the DJs who last are the ones who can actually DJ. Trends change, fake sets get exposed, but skill is forever

The pre-recorded DJ might get booked now, but they'll never have a legendary set because legendary sets are built on live decisions.

okay the reading the room ting is SO true. like last weekend I was playing and the energy was going a certain direction so I completely scrapped my planned transition and went into a whole different direction and the crowd went INSANE. you literally cannot do that with a pre-recorded set!!

THAT right there is the difference. That moment where you feel the crowd pulling in a direction and you pivot on the fly — that's a live, connected, REAL performance. No pre-recorded set can respond to the room because the room is unpredictable.

Those moments are what people remember. Nobody talks about the DJ who played a perfect-sounding set they didn't notice. People talk about the DJ who dropped THAT track at THAT moment and the whole room lost it.

FR FR. and another ting — if you're pre-recording sets you're never getting BETTER. you're not developing the skill. like what happens when someone puts you on a stage with CDJs and no laptop and says GO?? you're exposed!! you cant fake it in that situation

Exactly. And that situation comes up more often than people think — festivals where the backline is CDJs only, b2b sets where you HAVE to mix live with another DJ, technical failures where your laptop crashes and you need to improvise.

The fake-it-till-you-make-it approach works until it doesn't, and when it fails it fails publicly and permanently. One viral clip of a DJ clearly not mixing live and the reputation is done.

Honestly, the best thing you can do is just keep being undeniably good at what you do. The cream rises. Let the pre-recorded DJs have their moment — they plateau while you keep leveling up.
you know what I actually thought of something — should I call it out publicly?? like make a post or a video about fake DJs?? or is that just gonna make me look bitter

Hmm, I'd be careful with that. Calling out specific people will definitely make you look bitter and could burn bridges in the scene — promoters, venue owners, other DJs might see it as drama and avoid booking you.

But there IS a way to make the same point without being negative: make content that CELEBRATES live mixing. Film yourself doing real transitions, show the process, show the mistakes and recoveries. Let people see what real DJing looks like. That makes the point without attacking anyone.

Positive content that elevates the craft will always age better than call-out posts.

okay yea that's the smarter move. just show the WORK and let people draw their own conclusions. I like that. alright I feel better now. needed that vent session lol. the riddim of my mood is restored. bless!!

Ha, glad the vent helped reset the riddim! And yeah, showing the work is always the move. People respect authenticity and skill — just keep putting that out there.

Go kill your next set and let the real ones know what's up.
